item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[UNCONFIRMED EVENT REMOVED]

…was a stroke of genius.

item.title: Minor Edit
item.content:encoded:
Changed 'amoral' to 'immoral' in the section about murder. Amoral means something is considered morally neutral, neither right nor wrong.
The correct term is Immoral, meaning morally wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Iroquois Falls?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted when I got to the turn signal. I laughed very loudly in my very empty house.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it just me, or does the anomaly expunge concepts in locations with lots of people who rely on those concepts?

What if it expunged the concept of itself?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just gonna state for the record that this had some sort of wacky formatting error going on that sucked several paragraphs of the article into itself due to me dinging a footnote tag when editing for unrelated reasons. So for about a month or two, I think, a section of this article made no sense. That's what caused these errors and it's since been fixed (several days ago) and so has the color formatting error in the comment above yours (just now, because I'm not the sharpest). Sorry folks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't help but find the thought of people simply forgetting mundane concepts absolutely hilarious. What's a dog though?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the event of a full ED-K-Class Lethe Scenario 1 should have this document memorized.

Is this a typo? Who should have it memorised?

the latest. taking place

In Footnote 1. I think there's a word missing here.

As someone who lives near Felixstowe, the entry came as real 'what?!' moment. With the number of ships passing through there every day - an average of three thousand a year from the Port alone, not to mention the boats going down the Orwell, Stour, Deben - I imagine there'd be quite a stir. Just look at Google Maps and you can count the boats on the river. The Foundation would have their work cut out for them…

item.title: some formatting
item.content:encoded:
There's a couple of funky formatting things with this article, some may be up to the authors in terms of style, and some are just problems that may need fixed. I think maybe something may have been messed up in a recent update of the document. Here are a few I noticed:

- You attempt to put a footnote inside of the first footnote, and the formatting doesn't work
- There's something going on in the end of the third description bullet point. It says ##gray| and I'm not familiar enough with formatting to understand the intention.
- Footnote 7 is long enough that I feel like it should be some kind of addendum. Not only that, but it's long enough that I struggle to fit it all on my screen when I mouseover. That one's more of a stylistic thing though.

Great article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Im curious about whether this scip affects animals as well. What if dogs do not have the concept of dogs anymore? Are they gonna look in the mirror and go "whoa wtf is this floofy boi doing here"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, not recognising the existence of dogs is one thing, but the report seems to be at pains to point out that it affected the memory of all canines. If you live somewhere with wolves, that could be a bigger problem than simply freaking out at this random animal that's somehow got into your house. As well as for the police K9 department, or petty thieves who suddenly wonder why they've never thought of breaking into <someplace_guarded_by_watchdogs>, etc. And that would presumably extend to e.g. foxes as well, so anyone seeing one of those would be right on the phone to animal control / paranormal themed radio phone-ins to report sightings…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno on the second one. I see it as a bit creepier, like it might be a sign of the anti-meme at work. Like the anomaly is trying to hide evidence of itself by making the Foundation forget about one of its instances. Usually they'd retain the report, just mark it as currently being unconfirmed, at most strike it out. Particularly for SCPs like this where memory or information retention are in question, you don't delete things lightly, if your cognition is working correctly…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And as Felixstowe is one of the UK's busier container cargo ports, everyone there (which must include quite a number of dockworkers) permanently losing the concept of human operated marine vessels must have caused utter havoc, and put a good bit of national trade and security at risk…

Odd though, the usual stereotype of people living in the east of England is one of pointing and screaming at planes, not boats :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful idea. Wonderfully executed. Definitely up voted. My only qualm is all the instances, besides the first and the unconfirmed, don't add much to the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this.

The log of confirmed events was quite interesting and at times hilarious (see the Queens turn signal example). Additionally, the interview had a certain Monty Python feel to it in the constant reminder of dogs being a thing, which is a rather nice juxtaposition to the horror of very important information vanishing wholesale.

I do have to wonder if SCP-1422 would be considered such an event.

Either way, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is terrifying to think we could spontaneously forget anything and just never notice it, also go figure the only thing that can explain a new yorker driver is the unexplained (+1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor dogs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a brilliant idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are on the right track, there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It can even result in extinction of humanity if most people forget about concepts like eating, breeding, the need of disease prevention and/or cure, harvesting of food, and also the collapse of human society if concepts like working, transport, text and language are being forgotten. SCP-2000 would be needed to solve that, assuming that newly created human clones won't be affected. (Can you imagine the entire world population sitting on farmland looking at crops and starving to death)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a decent idea. After the contest is over, I'll consider adding something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like for there to be an example case where the description is incomprehensible. Ideally it wouldn't look like complete word salad, it would look like real sentences with normal English grammar, but totally meaningless. Something like SCP-058's dialogue but less poetic, and have some word be repeated several times that the reader can only assume is the name for whatever concept the entry is describing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fucking seconded. I literally burst out laughing at that line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I got to the boat one, I remembered on Phineas & Ferb where Doofenshmirtz "invented" the Buoyancy Operated Aquatic Transport.

(And since only 1 person in the Foundation was affected, it made it even more relevant to me.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Haha, I bet that the Queens thing would go unnoticed in California as easily. I feel like this is missing the spark of something that ties it together. It's nice to read, certainly, but it feels kind of like a bunch of disconnected events put together under the same name.

[Almost like a horse, but too soft.]
and YEAH THEY LOOK LIKE HORSES. I didn't get it until I searched up what Borzois look like, the fluffy little bastards.

[[UNCONFIRMED EVENT REMOVED]]
I like the existence of this. Something that shows the foundation is investigating, events are being analyzed and looked at rather than record and move on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I can't remember.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna hazard a guess that the [UNCONFIRMED EVENT REDACTED] section is in reference to the Augenlappen "incident" mentioned in 3484?

item.title: Re: Novote
item.content:encoded: It actually wasn't meant to be humorous. I figured people would do a tiny little "hmmph" at it, but otherwise it was just another event illustrating how it could affect public safety, even in seemingly minimal ways. But given the (unexpected) outcome of how people interpreted it, what you're saying makes sense.

item.title: Novote
item.content:encoded: I like the concept a lot, but the joke about losing Turn Signal awareness not affecting new york traffic feels very out of place and clashes with the tone of the rest of the article. Don;t get me wrong, its funny, but it throws off the mood of the whole article for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The one problem with this is that it's very similar, conceptually, to that recent article about the thing that made everyone forget how to communicate. Like, that could be a wide-scale subset of this phenomenon. But given that this is wider ranging, not to mention presented very differently, I think there's room for both. And if nothing else, I have to upvote for that interview, that was extremely well done. Further thoughts:

I like the implication that the Foundation logo is memetic.

[UNCONFIRMED EVENT REMOVED]

This is terrifying and I cannot entirely explain why.

I feel like this whole thing was written as a jab at New York drivers. XD +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good. My team almost did a concept very similar to this so I'm glad we came up with something else now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of a tale I wrote, but this is clearly more fleshed out and expanded on, and I'm interested to see where this whole scenario is going.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Location Affected: Queens, New York City, New York, USA

Description of Concept Affected: The existence of the turn signal indicator in passenger vehicles.

Fucking upvoted.
I'm loving everything this team is putting out.
Keep up the good work and best of luck!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having spent a couple of weeks working in Queens last year, I can confirm that the majority of the population remains effected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The implications of 2 million people forgetting how to use turn signals having zero impact on Queens traffic is one of the funniest parts of a very strong article.

item.title: author post
item.content:encoded: Welcome to the second installment in the Doomsday Contest series produced by myself and my team, WrongJohnSilver, LordStonefish, and Rimple. Many thanks to my team, as well as 9Volt, Sly161, minmin, and Fantem for draft help.
